title: 3.1.3 Organic Molecules
description: Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids.

questions.question: What is an organic molecule?
options: A compound that contains carbon and is found in living things
A molecule that contains only oxygen and hydrogen
Any molecule found in the environment
A molecule that does not contain carbon
questions.correctAnswer: A compound that contains carbon and is found in living things

questions.question: Why does carbon form the basis of organic life?
options: Carbon's ability to form large and complex molecules via covalent bonding
Carbon is the most abundant element
questions.correctAnswer: Carbon's ability to form large and complex molecules via covalent bonding

questions.question: What are the four principal groups of organic compounds?
options: Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids
questions.correctAnswer: Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids

questions.question: What are monomers?
options: Small, single molecular units that combine to form polymers
questions.correctAnswer: Small, single molecular units that combine to form polymers

questions.question: What type of bonds connect monomers to create polymers?
What are the monomers of proteins?
What are the monomers of lipids?
What are the monomers of carbohydrates?
What are the monomers of nucleic acids?
Which elements make up proteins?
options: C, H, O, N, and P
C, H, O, N, and sometimes P and S
questions.correctAnswer: C, H, O, N, and sometimes P and S

questions.question: Which elements make up carbohydrates?
Which elements make up nucleic acids?
options: C, H, O, N, and P
C, H, O, N, and S
questions.correctAnswer: C, H, O, N, and P

questions.question: What is the ratio of hydrogen to oxygen in carbohydrates?
What are the three main groups of carbohydrates?
What is a monosaccharide?
options: A long chain of sugar units
A molecule containing two sugar units
A molecule containing only one sugar unit
questions.correctAnswer: A molecule containing only one sugar unit

questions.question: How do monosaccharides dissolve in water?
options: They dissolve quickly, forming sweet-tasting solutions
They react with water to form acids
questions.correctAnswer: They dissolve quickly, forming sweet-tasting solutions

questions.question: What is the general formula of monosaccharides?
What is the ratio of carbon to hydrogen to oxygen in monosaccharides?
Which monosaccharides are classified as trioses?
What is the chemical formula for hexose sugar?
What is the relationship between glucose, galactose, and fructose?
options: They are isomers with the same chemical formula but different structures
questions.correctAnswer: They are isomers with the same chemical formula but different structures

questions.question: What is the chemical formula shared by glucose, galactose, and fructose?
What is the primary role of monosaccharides in living organisms?
What is released when carbon-hydrogen bonds in monosaccharides are broken?
options: A large amount of energy transferred to help make ATP
questions.correctAnswer: A large amount of energy transferred to help make ATP

questions.question: What is the most important monosaccharide in energy metabolism?
Which pentose sugar is used to make RNA and ATP?
Which pentose sugar is used to make DNA?
What is the formula for disaccharides?
What are disaccharides formed from?
What is maltose composed of?
What is lactose composed of?
What is sucrose composed of?
What is maltose the product of?
options: Breakdown of starch catalyzed by the enzyme amylase
questions.correctAnswer: Breakdown of starch catalyzed by the enzyme amylase

questions.question: What is the transport sugar in plants?
Which disaccharide is commonly found in milk?
What are the three most important polysaccharides?
What are polysaccharides made of?
Why is glucose converted to storage polysaccharides in cells?
options: To make the cell more acidic
Because glucose would dissolve and compromise the cell's osmotic properties
questions.correctAnswer: Because glucose would dissolve and compromise the cell's osmotic properties

questions.question: What type of reaction converts glucose to a storage polysaccharide?
What are the characteristics of a storage polysaccharide?
What is the storage polysaccharide in plants?
What is the storage polysaccharide in animals?
Where do plants store surplus glucose as starch?
options: In various plant components such as roots and seeds
questions.correctAnswer: In various plant components such as roots and seeds

questions.question: What is glycogen?
options: The storage form of glucose in humans and animals
The plant storage form of glucose
questions.correctAnswer: The storage form of glucose in humans and animals

questions.question: Glycogen is the animal analogue of which polysaccharide?
Where is glycogen often stored in the body?
What happens to glycogen if glucose levels fall?
options: It is broken down to release glucose
questions.correctAnswer: It is broken down to release glucose

questions.question: What percentage of liver mass can glycogen constitute in mammals?
What percentage of muscle mass can glycogen constitute in mammals?
What supplies energy for muscle contraction during exercise?
What is cellulose?
options: A naturally occurring biopolymer that is abundant
questions.correctAnswer: A naturally occurring biopolymer that is abundant

questions.question: What are plant cell walls mostly made of?
What gives cellulose its rigidity and high strength?
options: Every other glucose monomer is densely packed as long extended chains
questions.correctAnswer: Every other glucose monomer is densely packed as long extended chains

questions.question: What is dietary fibre that passes through the digestive system?
Why can herbivores digest cellulose while humans cannot?
options: Bacteria in their rumens secrete the enzyme cellulase to degrade cellulose
questions.correctAnswer: Bacteria in their rumens secrete the enzyme cellulase to degrade cellulose

questions.question: What enzyme degrades cellulose into glucose monomers?
The strength of cellulose fibres is roughly similar to which material?
What is one of the major functions of carbohydrates?
options: Primary source of energy for most organisms
questions.correctAnswer: Primary source of energy for most organisms

questions.question: Chitin is involved in the formation of which structure?
Cellulose is an important component of which diet?
Which reagent is used in Benedict's test for reducing sugars?
What colour is Benedict's reagent?
What colour change occurs in Benedict's test when a reducing sugar is present?
What is formed as a precipitate in Benedict's test?
Which common disaccharide is a non-reducing sugar?
What colour change is observed when iodine reagent is added to starch?
What are lipids?
options: Diverse groups of biomolecules insoluble in water but soluble in non-polar solvents
questions.correctAnswer: Diverse groups of biomolecules insoluble in water but soluble in non-polar solvents

questions.question: In which solvents are lipids soluble?
What is the state of fats at room temperature?
What is the state of oils at room temperature?
True lipids are organic compounds formed by what combining with alcohol?
What are the polymers that make up lipids?
What is the general formula for fatty acids?
What is the acidic group found in fatty acids called?
How many carbon atoms long is the hydrocarbon chain of a fatty acid typically?
What distinguishes unsaturated fatty acids from saturated fatty acids?
options: Unsaturated ones are solid at room temperature
Unsaturated ones have no carbon atoms
Unsaturated ones contain one or more double bonds
questions.correctAnswer: Unsaturated ones contain one or more double bonds

questions.question: What effect do double bonds have on fatty acids and lipids?
options: They make them insoluble in non-polar solvents
They have no effect on melting
They make them melt more easily
questions.correctAnswer: They make them melt more easily

questions.question: What describes a fatty acid with more than one double bond?
What describes a fatty acid with only one double bond?
What type of lipids are plant lipids often found as?
What type of lipids are animal lipids often found as?
Where is fat stored in the human body?
options: Just below the dermis of the skin and around the kidneys
questions.correctAnswer: Just below the dermis of the skin and around the kidneys

questions.question: What function does fat serve below the skin?
What replaces one of the three fatty acid molecules in a phospholipid?
What property does the phosphate group have in a phospholipid?
options: It is polar and dissolves in water
questions.correctAnswer: It is polar and dissolves in water

questions.question: The phosphate group in a phospholipid is described as what?
The two remaining tails in a phospholipid molecule are described as what?
How do phospholipids arrange themselves around a cell?
options: Hydrophilic heads lie in watery solutions outside the membrane, hydrophobic tails form an impermeable layer
questions.correctAnswer: Hydrophilic heads lie in watery solutions outside the membrane, hydrophobic tails form an impermeable layer

questions.question: What type of fats are stored in specialized cells called adipose cells?
options: Fats serve as major fuel store and poor conductors of heat
questions.correctAnswer: Fats serve as major fuel store and poor conductors of heat

questions.question: Lipids serve as precursors for the synthesis of which vitamins and substances?
options: Vitamin D, bile acids, and hormones
questions.correctAnswer: Vitamin D, bile acids, and hormones

questions.question: Which sex hormones are derived from cholesterol?
What gives fruits and leaves their shiny appearance?
What function do waxes serve on animals, birds, and insects?
options: Water barrier and protection against cold
questions.correctAnswer: Water barrier and protection against cold

questions.question: What elements do proteins contain?
options: Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen, and sometimes phosphorus and sulphur
questions.correctAnswer: Carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen, and sometimes phosphorus and sulphur

questions.question: What is the general formula for amino acids in proteins?
What are the monomers that make up proteins?
What percentage of the dry mass of most cells is protein?
What are the four groups bonded to a central carbon atom in an amino acid?
options: Carboxyl group, methyl group, oxygen atom, and R group
Amino group, hydroxyl group, hydrogen atom, and carbon chain
Hydroxyl group, phosphate group, sulfur group, and R group
Amino group, carboxylic acid group, hydrogen atom, and R group
questions.correctAnswer: Amino group, carboxylic acid group, hydrogen atom, and R group

questions.question: What is the R group in an amino acid?
options: The side chain that varies in different amino acids
Always the same in all amino acids
questions.correctAnswer: The side chain that varies in different amino acids

questions.question: How many different amino acids occur in the proteins of living organisms?
What type of bond joins amino acids together?
What is formed when two amino acids are linked together?
What is a molecule made of many amino acids linked by peptide bonds called?
Where do ribosomes function in relation to amino acids?
options: They are the sites where amino acids are joined together to form polypeptides
questions.correctAnswer: They are the sites where amino acids are joined together to form polypeptides

questions.question: What type of reaction breaks peptide bonds by adding water?
Where does hydrolysis of proteins occur naturally during digestion?
options: In the stomach and small intestine
questions.correctAnswer: In the stomach and small intestine

questions.question: What are globular proteins?
options: Proteins that have only structural roles
Proteins whose molecules curl up into a ball shape
Proteins that are insoluble in water
questions.correctAnswer: Proteins whose molecules curl up into a ball shape

questions.question: What are examples of globular proteins?
What are fibrous proteins?
options: Proteins that curl into ball shapes
Protein molecules that form long strands and are not usually soluble in water
questions.correctAnswer: Protein molecules that form long strands and are not usually soluble in water

questions.question: What are examples of fibrous proteins?
What is the function of structural proteins?
options: They serve as structural elements of connective tissues, bones, tendons, cartilages, nails, hair, and horns
questions.correctAnswer: They serve as structural elements of connective tissues, bones, tendons, cartilages, nails, hair, and horns

questions.question: What are enzymes?
options: Globular proteins that serve as biological catalysts
questions.correctAnswer: Globular proteins that serve as biological catalysts

questions.question: How do enzymes catalyze metabolic reactions?
options: By lowering the activation energy which increases the reaction rate
questions.correctAnswer: By lowering the activation energy which increases the reaction rate

questions.question: Which of the following are examples of protein enzymes?
options: DNA polymerase, lysozyme, nitrogenase, and lipase
questions.correctAnswer: DNA polymerase, lysozyme, nitrogenase, and lipase

questions.question: What are protein hormones?
options: Polypeptides made of long chains of linked amino acids that regulate physiological processes
questions.correctAnswer: Polypeptides made of long chains of linked amino acids that regulate physiological processes

questions.question: What are examples of protein hormones?
What are respiratory pigments in proteins?
options: Globular protein pigments that are typically water-soluble
questions.correctAnswer: Globular protein pigments that are typically water-soluble

questions.question: What is the function of myoglobin?
options: It transports oxygen in the blood
It provides oxygen to working muscles
questions.correctAnswer: It provides oxygen to working muscles

questions.question: What is the function of haemoglobin?
options: It provides oxygen to working muscles
It transports oxygen to all tissues and organs via the blood
questions.correctAnswer: It transports oxygen to all tissues and organs via the blood

questions.question: What are transport proteins?
options: Cell membrane components that create channels to transport specific molecules
questions.correctAnswer: Cell membrane components that create channels to transport specific molecules

questions.question: What are examples of transport proteins?
options: Serum albumin, channel proteins, and carrier proteins
questions.correctAnswer: Serum albumin, channel proteins, and carrier proteins

questions.question: What are motor proteins involved in?
What are examples of motor proteins?
What is the function of storage proteins?
options: They serve as a storage reserve for amino acids and metal ions
questions.correctAnswer: They serve as a storage reserve for amino acids and metal ions

questions.question: What are examples of storage proteins?
Which organisms most commonly produce protein toxins?
How do protein toxins from bacteria help the bacteria?
options: They kill the host organism by causing cytotoxicity
questions.correctAnswer: They kill the host organism by causing cytotoxicity

questions.question: What colour appears in the Biuret test when protein is present?
What does the Biuret test detect in an alkaline solution?
What does the Xanthoproteic test identify in proteins?
options: Amino acids containing phenolic or indolic groups like phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan
questions.correctAnswer: Amino acids containing phenolic or indolic groups like phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan

questions.question: What is added to the sample in the Xanthoproteic test?
options: Concentrated nitric acid followed by NaOH
questions.correctAnswer: Concentrated nitric acid followed by NaOH

questions.question: What are nucleic acids?
options: Chemical molecules made of phosphoric acid, sugars, and organic bases
questions.correctAnswer: Chemical molecules made of phosphoric acid, sugars, and organic bases

questions.question: What are the two main types of nucleic acids?
What are the smaller molecules that form DNA and RNA called?
In most organisms, where is genetic information carried?
options: In the nucleotide sequences of DNA
In the fatty acid chains of lipids
In the amino acid sequences of proteins
questions.correctAnswer: In the nucleotide sequences of DNA

questions.question: What are genes?
options: Stretches of DNA that code for RNA and amino acids and therefore proteins
questions.correctAnswer: Stretches of DNA that code for RNA and amino acids and therefore proteins

questions.question: What are the three components of a nucleotide?
options: A nitrogen-containing base, a pentose sugar, and a phosphate group
Amino group, carboxyl group, and R group
questions.correctAnswer: A nitrogen-containing base, a pentose sugar, and a phosphate group

questions.question: How many different nitrogen-containing bases are found in DNA and RNA?
What are the four nitrogenous bases in DNA?
Which nitrogenous base is present in RNA instead of thymine?
Which base is found in DNA but replaced by uracil in RNA?
What determines the hereditary function of nucleic acids?
options: The order and composition of different nucleotide sequences
questions.correctAnswer: The order and composition of different nucleotide sequences

questions.question: What pentose sugar is found in RNA?
What pentose sugar is found in DNA?
How does deoxyribose differ from ribose?
options: It has no oxygen atom on its second carbon atom
It has an extra oxygen atom
It has an extra carbon atom
questions.correctAnswer: It has no oxygen atom on its second carbon atom

questions.question: What is the structure of DNA?
options: Double helix made of two strands of nucleotides
questions.correctAnswer: Double helix made of two strands of nucleotides

questions.question: What is the structure of RNA?
What are the three types of RNA?
What is the function of DNA as genetic material?
options: It stores all information required to be transferred to the next generation and has the property of replication
questions.correctAnswer: It stores all information required to be transferred to the next generation and has the property of replication

questions.question: What is the process of copying DNA information to mRNA called?
What is the process of translating the code in mRNA into amino acid sequences called?
What is the role of mRNA?
options: It provides amino acids for protein synthesis
It moves the genetic code from DNA to ribosomes
questions.correctAnswer: It moves the genetic code from DNA to ribosomes

questions.question: What is the role of tRNA?
options: It helps protein synthesis by providing a source of amino acids
It carries genetic code from DNA
questions.correctAnswer: It helps protein synthesis by providing a source of amino acids

questions.question: What is the role of rRNA?
options: It forms a complex with proteins making the structure of the ribosome
questions.correctAnswer: It forms a complex with proteins making the structure of the ribosome

questions.question: In some viruses, what acts as genetic material instead of DNA?
What is an RNA molecule with enzymatic activity called?
Compared to DNA, how stable is RNA?
options: Less stable and degraded quite quickly
RNA is not degraded at all
questions.correctAnswer: Less stable and degraded quite quickly

questions.question: How does the size of DNA compare to RNA?
options: DNA is huge allowing it to carry code for many proteins while RNA is much smaller
questions.correctAnswer: DNA is huge allowing it to carry code for many proteins while RNA is much smaller

questions.question: Which element distinguishes nucleic acids from carbohydrates in terms of composition?
Glycogen is a polysaccharide food reserve of which organisms?
What type of starch-digesting enzyme is found in saliva?
What problem arises when excess glucose accumulates in cells without being stored as polysaccharide?
options: It would dissolve and compromise the cell's osmotic properties
questions.correctAnswer: It would dissolve and compromise the cell's osmotic properties

questions.question: What does the cell wall of plants contain multiple layers of?
options: Cellulose fibres that run in different directions to boost strength
questions.correctAnswer: Cellulose fibres that run in different directions to boost strength

questions.question: What happens to a plant cell without a cell wall in a dilute fluid?
Despite their strength, what characteristic do cellulose fibres have?
options: They are porous, allowing water and solutes to pass through
questions.correctAnswer: They are porous, allowing water and solutes to pass through

questions.question: What hormones of the adrenal cortex are derived from cholesterol?
How many strands does DNA have?
How many strands does RNA have?
